list.title: Your pets
list.subtitle: Add a profile for each animal you feed so meal plans match their species, age, and conditions.

pancreatitis.label: Pancreatitis
pancreatitis.help: Lean proteins only, no fatty cuts.

pets.confirmDeleteHelp: This permanently removes the pet and unlinks them from any meal plans.

data.title: Backup & restore
data.help: Your pets and meal plans live only on this device. Export a backup file you can load on another phone or after clearing storage.
data.nothingToExport: Nothing to export yet — add a pet or a plan first.
data.exportSuccess: Saved backup with {{pets}} pet(s) and {{plans}} plan(s).
data.importPartial: Imported {{pets}} pet(s) and {{plans}} plan(s). {{skipped}} entry(ies) skipped (invalid).

importConfirm.replaceOrMerge:
Replace your current pets and plans with the backup?

OK = replace everything.
Cancel = merge (keep both, same-name items updated).

error.fileTooLarge: Backup is unexpectedly large. Use a file under 5 MB.
error.readFailed: Couldn't read the file. Try again.
error.notPawcookBackup: That doesn't look like a PawCook backup.
error.unsupportedVersion: Backup is from a newer version of PawCook. Update the app first.

landing.title: Meal plans
landing.subtitle: Generate a weekly, biweekly, or monthly plan and the shopping list you can take to the store.
landing.emptyDescription: Create a weekly plan and PawCook builds the shopping list and bag schedule for you automatically.
landing.emptyNoPetsDescription: Pick a starter pack and we'll set up your pets and your first plan in a couple of clicks.

new.title: New plan
new.subtitle: Pick your pets and how many days you're planning for. We'll handle the rest.

needPet.title: Add a pet first.
needPet.description: Add a pet profile first — the plan is built around your pet's weight, age, and diet.

wizard.petsHelp: Pick one or more. Mixed-species plans share a single shopping list.
wizard.editSubtitle: Tweak anything and save to overwrite — your shopping check marks stay.
wizard.petRemoved: {{count}} pet(s) were removed from this plan because their profile no longer exists.

sourcing.varietyHelp: How much rotation across proteins each week.
sourcing.accessHelp: Pick what's realistic for your usual stores.
sourcing.meatsHelp: Pick the proteins you want — leave empty to let the planner choose any.
sourcing.veggiesHelp: Pick the produce you want — leave empty to let the planner choose any.
sourcing.methodHelp: We remember this so "Cook" buttons in the shopping list open the calculator already set up your way.
sourcing.includeOrgansHelp: Off = no organs in the shopping list. Heads up: raw-style diets rely on organs for vitamin A and (for cats) taurine.
sourcing.bagSizeHelp: How many days of meals go in each sous-vide bag. Bigger bags = less plastic and fewer cook sessions, but they sit in the freezer longer.
sourcing.bagSizeCaption: One protein per bag · veggies in their own bags
sourcing.bagSizeWarn: Some bags will sit frozen for ~3 weeks. Safe, but flavour degrades — consider 2 or 3.
sourcing.cookAheadHelp: How many days each batch should cover. Bigger windows = fewer cook sessions and less plastic; the planner groups consecutive days of the same protein and same veggie so each bag is full.
sourcing.cookAheadCapSafety: Cooked meat keeps 3 days after thaw.
sourcing.cookAheadCaption: One protein per bag · veggies in their own bags
sourcing.cookAheadWarn: Some bags will sit frozen for ~3 weeks. Safe, but flavour fades — try a shorter window.
sourcing.cookAheadStatsSaving: {{bags}} bags · {{sessions}} cook session(s) · saves {{saved}} vs daily
sourcing.simpleMealsHelp: One protein per meal — same bag for the whole household. Cats get a daily taurine + cod liver oil supplement card to fill the gap. Turn off for the full multi-component recipe (organs, seafood add-ins, per-pet macros).
sourcing.tinyHouseholdHint: Tiny household (~{{grams}} g/day) — try Up to 3 days to avoid runt-sized bags.
sourcing.veggieDetailHelp: Off (recommended): one mixed batch per cook day — carrots, sweet potato, green beans all in the same pot. On: each veggie gets its own cook session in the plan, with its own bag schedule.

coverage.passedHelp: No nutrition flags surfaced for the pets in this plan.

shopping.empty: Generate a plan and we'll build the bag list.
shopping.exportFooter: PawCook shopping list — not veterinary advice.
shopping.surplusDoNotFeed: {{need}} needed – {{extra}} do not feed, freeze for next plan
shopping.yieldAdjustedHint: Buy includes cooking shrinkage — yields ~{{c}} g cooked.
shopping.emptyHelp: This plan has nothing to buy yet. Edit the plan to add proteins or vegetables.

cookingPlan.emptyHelp: Switch the plan to sous-vide and we'll build a date-stamped bag schedule.
cookingPlan.noBagsHelp: Nothing to batch here — everything in this plan is cooked fresh on the day.
cookingPlan.summary: {{bags}} bags · {{sessions}} cook day(s) · one protein per bag
cookingPlan.rotationNote: Protein rotates — bag dates may be non-consecutive.
cookingPlan.freshHelp: These don't freeze well batched. Handle daily or per recipe.
cookingPlan.frozenQualityGroup: {{count}} bag(s) sit over 30 days frozen — safe, but flavour fades. Shorten the cook-ahead next plan.
cookingPlan.supplementCardsHelp: Too small to bag — pinch into the bowl daily instead. Cooking these would mean opening 20 bags for 6 g of carrot.
cookingPlan.veggieSessionsHelp: Each card groups what to cook together on one day. Tap Plan bags for an exact bag-by-bag workflow.

sharpie.title: Label bag {{n}}/{{total}}
sharpie.intro: Copy this onto the bag with a permanent marker. One bag at a time.
sharpie.tipTape: Reusable silicone bag? Stick a strip of masking tape on it and write on that.

mealPlan.confirmDelete: Delete plan "{{name}}"? This can't be undone.

toast.generateFailed: Could not generate the plan — try different settings.

refusal.title: Plan not generated
refusal.subtitle: Fix the issues below, then try again. If this diet is vet-prescribed, open the pet's profile and enable vet-prescription mode to relax these checks.
refusal.vetOverrideHint: Can be relaxed with a matching vet prescription.

rule.zero_protein: Diet has 0% protein — every dog needs dietary protein.
rule.protein_below_nrc_min: Protein is {{value}}% — below the FEDIAF/NRC adult-maintenance minimum of {{threshold}}%.
rule.cooked_bone: Diet includes cooked bone, which splinters and can cause choking or GI perforation. Switch the cooking method to raw or remove bone from the protein composition.
rule.ca_p_inverted: Ca:P ratio {{value}}:1 is outside the AAFCO safe range of {{threshold}}:1 to 2:1.
rule.high_fat_pancreatitis: Fat is {{value}}% — above the pancreatitis-safe ceiling of {{threshold}}%.
rule.expired_vet_review: The vet prescription's review date has passed. Re-confirm the plan with the vet before generating new plans.

mealPlan.confirmDeleteHelp: Deleting a plan can’t be undone. Your saved pet profiles will not be affected.

supplements.title: Daily supplements
supplements.help: What the simple-meals plan doesn't cover. Set these up once and dose with breakfast.

taurine.label: Taurine powder
taurine.rationale: Cooking destroys ~20% of taurine. Deficit causes irreversible blindness + dilated cardiomyopathy.

codLiver.label: Cod liver oil
codLiver.rationale: Covers pre-formed vitamin A and omega-3 (EPA/DHA) in one dose — what the dropped organ + seafood slots no longer provide.

calcium.label: Calcium citrate (or ground eggshell)
calcium.dose: {{mg}} mg per day (~½ tsp ground eggshell per ~454 g meat)
calcium.rationale: Cooked meals are calcium-deficient without bone — this closes the gap.

starter.title: Pick a starter pack
starter.subtitle: Skip the long form. Pick the household that matches yours and we'll seed the pets and the plan — you can tweak anything after.
starter.manualTitle: Prefer to set things up yourself?
starter.manualDescription: Add a pet from scratch and walk through the full wizard.

single_adult_dog.title: Single adult dog
single_adult_dog.description: One adult dog, easy supermarket proteins, weekend cook session. Best for busy households starting fresh.

multi_pet_dog_cat.title: Dog and cat household
multi_pet_dog_cat.description: Both pets eat the same cooked protein you prep once. Cat gets a daily taurine and cod liver oil supplement card so the simple plan stays safe.

single_indoor_cat.title: Single indoor cat
single_indoor_cat.description: One indoor cat on a cooked-carnivore diet. Small batches, weekday stovetop cook, taurine supplement included.

senior_dog_low_activity.title: Senior dog, low activity
senior_dog_low_activity.description: Older dog with joint support. Slow-cooker prep for tender proteins, biweekly plan to keep cook days infrequent.

welcome.title: Welcome to your plan
welcome.body: Here's a 30-second tour of what's here. Skip any time.

meals.title: Meals
meals.body: Day-by-day portions per pet — grams, kcal, and the protein for each meal.

supplement.title: Daily supplement
supplement.body: When the plan simplifies meals, dropped nutrients show up here as a named daily dose — not silently omitted.

shopping.title: Shopping list
shopping.body: Grouped by store section. Tap an item to check it off as you shop.

cooking.title: Cooking plan
cooking.body: Date-stamped bag schedule for sous-vide. Cook once, feed for days.

kidney.lowPhosphorus: Kidney condition: keep phosphorus on the lower end of the plan.
kidney.avoidOrgans: Kidney condition: avoid organ-heavy days; rotate liver out when possible.

pancreatitis.leanOnly: Pancreatitis: lean proteins only — no duck, lamb, or fatty cuts.

diabetes.lowCarb: Diabetes: keep starches and fruits low; favour proteins and fibrous veg.

joint.omega3: Joint support: prioritise omega-3 sources (sardines, mackerel, salmon).

gi.bland: Sensitive stomach: lean white proteins (chicken, turkey, rabbit) digest more easily.

obese.kcalReduce: Overweight: calorie target is reduced — track body condition weekly.

allergy.respectList: Allergens listed on this pet's profile are excluded from the plan.

cooking.title: Cooking Calculator
cooking.subtitle: Calculate safe cooking time and temperature for your dog's food.
cooking.placeholder: Fill in the form above and tap

instr.sous_vide: Seal meat in vacuum bags. Preheat water bath to 74 °C (165 °F). Submerge bags and cook for the full calculated time. Verify internal temperature with a probe thermometer before serving.
instr.oven: Preheat oven to 120 °C (248 °F). Place meat in a covered roasting dish; add a splash of water or broth to prevent drying. Check with a probe thermometer — target 74 °C (165 °F) internal temperature.
instr.stovetop_low: Bring a pot of water to a gentle simmer (not a rolling boil). Add meat and stir regularly — every 2 min for minced, every 5 min for cubed or larger cuts. Remove from heat when internal temperature reaches 74 °C (165 °F). Include the cooking broth in the meal.
instr.slow_cooker: Place meat in the slow cooker and add ½ cup water or broth. Set to LOW — do not use HIGH as rapid temperature swings increase bacterial risk. Verify 74 °C (165 °F) internal temperature before serving.
instr.pressure_cooker: Place meat in the pressure cooker with ½ cup water or broth. Lock the lid and cook at high pressure for the calculated time. Use a natural release of at least 10 minutes before opening, then verify 74 °C (165 °F) internal temperature. Include the cooking liquid in the meal.

store.frozen: Cooked, frozen: up to 3 months in airtight containers.
store.cooling: Cool from 60 °C → 21 °C within 2 hours, then to ≤4 °C within 4 more hours.

warn.noBones: Never include cooked bones in any recipe — they splinter and can cause choking or GI perforation.
warn.thermometer: Always verify internal temperature with a meat thermometer.
warn.pork: Pork must always be fully cooked — raw pork carries Trichinella risk.
warn.salmon: Salmon should only be fed raw if frozen for ≥7 days first (Neorickettsia risk).
warn.catThiaminase: For cats: thiaminase in raw fish destroys thiamine. Always cook fish thoroughly before feeding to a cat.

nutrient.sous_vide: Sous-vide preserves the most water-soluble vitamins (B-complex, Vit C). Include all bag juices in the meal.
nutrient.oven: Some B vitamins are lost to pan drippings — include all pan juices in the meal for full nutritional value.
nutrient.stovetop_low: B vitamins leach into the cooking water — always include the broth in the meal.
nutrient.slow_cooker: Long, gentle cooking retains most nutrients in the liquid — serve the broth alongside the meat.
nutrient.pressure_cooker: Pressure cooking retains more water-soluble vitamins than open boiling and breaks down connective tissue. Always include the cooking liquid in the meal.

cooking.placeholderHint: Pick a meat. Pick a method. We handle the math.
cooking.catSupplementsBody: Heat destroys taurine, B-vitamins (thiamine, niacin) and some vitamin E. Add a feline supplement stack — taurine 250–500 mg, B-complex, vitamin E — after the meal cools.
cooking.subtitle_cat: Safe cooking time and temperature for your cat's food. Remember to add taurine + B-complex after cooking.
cooking.prefilledHelp: We carried over the meat and total weight from “{{plan}}”. Tweak anything before calculating.
cooking.veggieGuideHelp: Quick reference for cooking the produce side of the meal — slightly tender, never mushy.

vegGuide.help: Pick the appliance you actually use — we translate the recommendation to your method and tell you when something needs a different approach.
vegGuide.sousVideBathNote: Vegetables need a hotter bath than meat. Cook them first at 85 °C, then drop the temperature for your protein — one appliance, two stages.
vegGuide.fellBackTo: kept as {{method}} — {{appliance}} not suitable for this veg

bagStrategy.title: Bag strategy
bagStrategy.help: Feeds {{pets}} pet(s) for {{days}} days total. After cooking, keep 1 bag in the fridge (use within {{fridge}} days) and freeze the other {{frozen}}. Thaw a bag overnight before serving.
bagStrategy.runtTip: {{days}} days per bag would give you {{bags}} bags instead of {{current}} — same food, less plastic. Tap to switch.
bagStrategy.floorReason: Minimum {{bags}} bags for {{days}} days — cooked meat only keeps {{fridge}} days thawed.
bagStrategy.hero: {{bags}} bags × {{kg}} kg — one bag per {{meat}} serve
bagStrategy.helpPlan: {{bags}} {{meat}} bag(s) across {{days}} days for {{pets}} pet(s). Cook all {{totalKg}} kg at once — fridge 1 bag (use within {{fridge}} days of thaw), freeze the rest. Thaw each bag the night before its serve day.
bagStrategy.helpStandalone: {{bags}} bag(s) of {{kg}} kg for {{pets}} pet(s). Fridge 1, freeze the rest. Thaw each bag overnight before use.
bagStrategy.mergeHint: Saves {{saved}} cook session(s) — adjacent serves fit within the {{fridge}}-day fridge window after thaw.
bagStrategy.bagRowSupplement: serves {{serveDates}} · cook fresh, skip the bag

cooking.exportFooter: PawCook — not veterinary advice. Use a meat thermometer to verify safe internal temperature.

meatSwitcher.label: Cook a different meat from this plan

carrots.recommendedNotes: Slice 1 cm thick. Steam until fork-tender; retains beta-carotene better than boiling.
carrots.recommendedReason: Steaming preserves beta-carotene and vitamin C while fully breaking down cell walls dogs can't digest raw.

sous_vide.notes: Slice 3–5 mm so the bag conforms. Use a dedicated 85 °C bath — vegetables will not soften at meat-cooking temperatures.

oven.notes: Cube 2 cm and toss in a little oil. Roasting concentrates beta-carotene more than steam.

slow_cooker.warning: Slow cookers turn carrots mushy — fine for a stew, less ideal as a standalone side.

pressure_cooker.notes: 1 cm chunks, high pressure with 1 cup water, quick release.

pumpkin.recommendedNotes: Cube 2–3 cm. Roast plain — no sugar, no spice. Mash before serving.
pumpkin.recommendedReason: Roasting drives off water and concentrates the soluble fiber that regulates stool — the main reason pumpkin is in the bowl.

stovetop_low.notes: Steam over a colander; boiling waterlogs it and dilutes the fiber benefit.

slow_cooker.warning: Will be very wet — drain or reduce liquid before mashing.

pressure_cooker.notes: 2 cm chunks, high pressure, natural release.

sweet_potato.recommendedNotes: Always cooked — raw sweet potato is hard to digest. Cube or bake whole and mash.
sweet_potato.recommendedReason: Roasting caramelizes starch into easily digestible sugars and is the most palatable preparation. Sous-vide produces a sweeter result thanks to slow enzymatic conversion, if you have the time.
sweet_potato.rawBlockedReason: Raw sweet potato contains trypsin inhibitors and is essentially indigestible.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: cap at 1 tablespoon per meal — high starch.

sous_vide.notes: 1 cm cubes, dedicated 85 °C bath. Beta-amylase converts starch to maltose — comes out noticeably sweeter than baked.

stovetop_low.notes: Steam cubed; boiling raises the glycemic load.

pressure_cooker.notes: 2 cm chunks, high pressure, natural release.

broccoli.recommendedNotes: Florets only, no thick stems. Keep al dente — overcooking destroys vitamin C.
broccoli.recommendedReason: Short steaming keeps vitamin C and sulforaphane intact while softening the florets enough for canine digestion.

sous_vide.notes: Small florets, single layer in the bag. Sulfur smell will be intense on opening — this is normal.
sous_vide.warning: Florets go olive-drab in a sealed bag; nutritionally fine, just visually unappealing.

oven.warning: Some dogs refuse the Maillard browning on brassicas — try a small batch first.

pressure_cooker.notes: Low pressure — the come-up cooks them. Quick-release immediately or they turn to mush.

spinach.recommendedNotes: Wilt briefly in boiling water, drain, chop. Reduces oxalates slightly.
spinach.recommendedReason: A brief boil-and-drain reduces oxalates that interfere with calcium absorption, while preserving folate and iron.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: avoid, or limit to a teaspoon weekly — oxalate load is a concern for stone-prone breeds (Burmese, Persian, Himalayan).

green_beans.recommendedNotes: Trim ends. Steam until bright green and tender-crisp. Can be served raw too.
green_beans.recommendedReason: Steaming softens the pod fiber without leaching vitamin K and manganese — the nutrients that make green beans a go-to filler for weight-loss diets.

sous_vide.notes: Trim ends, keep whole — they hold shape beautifully.

oven.warning: Tends to dry them out — pets usually prefer steamed.

zucchini.recommendedNotes: Slice or cube. Safe raw — cooking only softens for picky eaters.
zucchini.recommendedReason: Light steaming softens for picky eaters without destroying the high water content that helps hydration.

sous_vide.warning: Releases a lot of water into the bag — drain before serving.

slow_cooker.warning: Turns to mush — fine in a stew, not as a side.

pressure_cooker.notes: Low pressure, quick release — overcooks instantly.

peas.recommendedNotes: Fresh or frozen (thaw first). Never use canned (added sodium).
peas.recommendedReason: Quick steam preserves lutein and B-vitamins that boiling leaches out.

pressure_cooker.notes: Low pressure, quick release. Frozen peas only need to thaw.

kale.recommendedNotes: Strip leaves from tough stems, chop fine. Sparingly — oxalates + thiocyanates.
kale.recommendedReason: Brief steaming deactivates the goitrogenic enzymes that interfere with thyroid function while keeping iron and vitamin K available.
kale.rawBlockedReason: Raw kale's enzymes activate goitrogenic thiocyanates and make oxalate more bioavailable.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: avoid if on thyroid medication. Otherwise occasional only.

sous_vide.warning: Texture goes slick in a sealed bag — most pets prefer it steamed.

oven.warning: This is effectively a kale-chip prep — treat-portion, not a side.

pressure_cooker.notes: Low pressure — best method for reducing oxalates in kale, beets and spinach.

parsnips.recommendedNotes: Peel and cube 2 cm. Cook fully — raw parsnips are very fibrous.
parsnips.recommendedReason: Steam fully softens the dense fiber and breaks down furanocoumarins that irritate the gut when raw.
parsnips.rawBlockedReason: Raw parsnips contain furanocoumarins (psoralens) — phototoxic and GI-irritating.

sous_vide.notes: 1–2 cm chunks. Below 85 °C they stay fibrous — use the higher 'firm root' bath temperature.

pressure_cooker.notes: 2 cm chunks, high pressure, natural release.

beets.recommendedNotes: Trim greens, scrub. Cook whole then peel; expect colored stool — harmless.
beets.recommendedReason: Steaming retains betalains and folate that boiling washes away — these are the entire reason beets are in the bowl.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: avoid — high oxalate load is a concern for stone-prone breeds.

sous_vide.notes: Whole at 2–3 hr (skin slips off after), or 2 cm cubes at 90 min. Best method for retaining betalains.

oven.notes: Wrap whole in foil with a splash of water. Concentrates flavor.

stovetop_low.warning: If you boil, include the cooking water in the bowl — that's where the betalain antioxidants ended up.

pressure_cooker.notes: Whole, high pressure, natural release. Pressure breaks down oxalates better than steaming.

butternut_squash.recommendedNotes: Halve, scoop seeds. Roast cut-side down on parchment. Mash flesh.
butternut_squash.recommendedReason: Roasting concentrates the beta-carotene and produces the sweetness that makes squash one of the most palatable veg additions.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: cap at 1 tablespoon per meal — high starch.

sous_vide.notes: 2 cm cubes, single layer. Comes out silky.

stovetop_low.notes: Steam cubed; boiling waterlogs the flesh.

slow_cooker.warning: Will be very wet — drain or reduce liquid before mashing.

pressure_cooker.notes: 2 cm chunks, high pressure, natural release.

cucumber.recommendedNotes: Serve raw — high water content. Slice into sticks; cooking ruins texture.
cucumber.recommendedReason: Cucumber is 95% water — cooking serves no purpose and destroys the hydration and crunch that are its only contribution.
cucumber.cookingBlockedReason: Cooking turns cucumber into a translucent slime pets refuse — raw is the only sensible preparation.

celery.recommendedNotes: Chop finely into 5 mm pieces to prevent choking. Strings are hard to digest.
celery.recommendedReason: Raw celery preserves apigenin and its mild diuretic effect — just chop fine to avoid string-related choking.

speciesNotes.cat: Small cats and dogs under 10 kg: consider steamed instead — finer chop reduces choking risk.

sous_vide.notes: Softens the strings that otherwise pass through whole.

asparagus.recommendedNotes: Trim woody ends. Steam until tender. Slice into bite-size pieces before serving.
asparagus.recommendedReason: Steam softens the woody fibers while preserving folate — folate is asparagus's main nutritional contribution.

sous_vide.notes: Lay flat in the bag, single layer.

brussels_sprouts.recommendedNotes: Halve sprouts, steam until tender. Limit portion size — known to cause gas.
brussels_sprouts.recommendedReason: Steaming deactivates the thiocyanates that suppress thyroid function while keeping the texture pets actually eat.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: avoid if on thyroid medication.

sous_vide.warning: Sulfur smell concentrates in a sealed bag — open with the lid pointed away.

oven.notes: Halve, cut-side down. Many dogs prefer this caramelized form to steamed.

pressure_cooker.notes: Low pressure, quick release. Longer and they turn to mush.

bagPlanner.title: Plan veggie bags
bagPlanner.subtitle: Type what you have on hand — pick a cut, get a bag-by-bag workflow you can actually follow.
bagPlanner.householdHelper: Total grams across all pets per day. Used to size bags.
bagPlanner.fromFrozenHelper: Adds the from-frozen minutes per cut (when the spec defines them).
bagPlanner.emptyHint: Enter how many grams of each veggie you have to see your bag plan.
bagPlanner.mixedNotPossible: These veggies cannot co-cook in one session — switch to Separate, or change cuts/method so they can share the pot.
bagPlanner.stepPortionSingle: Portion all {{raw}} g raw ({{cooked}} g cooked) into 1 bag (fits ~{{size}} g container).
bagPlanner.stepPortionEven: Portion into {{n}} bags of {{cooked}} g cooked (~{{raw}} g raw each).
bagPlanner.stepPortionUneven: Portion into {{n}} bags: {{full}} × {{cooked}} g + 1 × {{lastCooked}} g cooked (~{{raw}} g raw per full bag, {{lastRaw}} g raw for the last).
bagPlanner.stepVacuumSeal: Vacuum-seal each bag flat in a single layer; freeze immediately.
bagPlanner.stepSiliconeTray: Pour into silicone trays; freeze; pop cubes into a freezer bag for portion-on-demand.
bagPlanner.stepFreezerBag: Bag each portion flat; freeze; snap-off portions as you go.
bagPlanner.stepRigid: Pack into containers; refrigerate (no freezing). Use within 3–4 days.
bagPlanner.noSpec: This method is not supported for this veggie — change cut or method.
bagPlanner.stepCool: Cool to ≤21 °C before fridge (within 2 h); ice-bath leafy/cruciferous.
bagPlanner.headroomNote: Last bag has {{g}} g spare headroom — you can top up with the next cook or freeze as-is.

nutrition.title: Nutrition Calculator
nutrition.subtitle: Calculate daily portions based on your dog's profile.

notes.rawDiet: Advanced raw diet — bone content provides natural calcium; never substitute cooked bones.
notes.realAncestralOmega: Whole seafood (sardines, mackerel) balances omega-3:6. Use small wild-caught fish to limit mercury.
notes.taurineHeartsNote: Raw diet with hearts assumed — natural taurine source. Aim for ≥30% of muscle meat from hearts.
notes.arachidonicCookedNote: Cooked diet: ensure animal fat (chicken skin, egg yolk) is included for arachidonic acid.
notes.cookedCatSupplementsNote: Cooked cat diets need taurine + B-complex + vitamin E supplementation, added after cooking.
notes.hydrationCatNote: Cats have weak thirst drive — feed wet food, add water or bone broth, monitor urine output.

diets.balanced_cooked: Lean cooked (vet-recommended baseline) — 50% protein / 30% veg / 20% starch

nutrition.vetDisclaimer: This tool provides general guidance only. Consult a board-certified veterinary nutritionist (ACVN) for long-term homemade feeding.

custom.title: Your custom mix
custom.compositionHelp: Sub-share of the protein portion. Useful when following PMR-style ratios.

summary.meatHeavy: Mostly meat. Add a calcium source — there's no bone in this mix.
summary.highMeat: Meat-led, with a small vegetable share. Common vet-prescribed cooked pattern.
summary.balancedMeat: Balanced meat-and-plant split — middle-of-the-road for adult dogs.
summary.vegHeavy: Vegetable-heavy. Make sure your protein is dense enough to meet daily needs.
summary.mixed: Mixed plate. Confirm the totals add up to 100%.

warn.lowProtein: Protein is below the FEDIAF/NRC adult minimum (~18%). Confirm with your vet.
warn.noCalcium: No calcium source for an all-meat plan. Add bone, eggshell, or a supplement.
warn.highBone: Bone share above 15% may cause constipation.

custom.vetPrescribedHelp: Marking a diet vet-prescribed lets the planner relax overridable safety checks that would otherwise block plans (e.g. low protein in advanced CKD). Toxicology rules (cooked bone, allium, xylitol, etc.) stay enforced.

cooking.nonCanonical: {{preset}} is traditionally fed raw — make sure your cooked recipe still delivers complete nutrition (especially calcium).
cooking.pmrLockBody: PMR's calcium comes from raw bone (10% of the diet). Cooked bones splinter and can cause choking or GI perforation. Switch to BARF cooked, or build a custom diet with a calcium supplement.

nutrition.placeholder: Fill in your dog's profile and tap

warnings.caPLow: Ca:P ratio is {{ratio}}:1 — below the AAFCO safe minimum of 1:1. Add ~{{calcium}} mg supplemental calcium daily.
warnings.caPHigh: Ca:P ratio is {{ratio}}:1 — above the AAFCO safe maximum of 2:1. Reduce bone content or balance with low-calcium protein.
warnings.puppyCaP: Growing puppies are sensitive to high Ca:P — keep under 1.6:1 to avoid skeletal disease.
warnings.cookedCaDeficient: Cooked diets are calcium-deficient by default. Add ~{{calcium}} mg Ca (≈½ tsp ground eggshell per ~454 g meat, or use a balancer).
warnings.lowTaurine: Taurine is missing or low. Cooking destroys taurine; supplementation is mandatory. Deficiency causes irreversible blindness and dilated cardiomyopathy.
warnings.lowArachidonic: Arachidonic acid is too low. Cats cannot synthesize it — must come from animal fat or egg yolk.
warnings.lowArginine: Arginine is too low. A single arginine-deficient meal can cause acute hyperammonemia in cats.
warnings.vitAToxicity: Liver share ({{liverPct}}%) exceeds safe ceiling. Reduce to avoid hypervitaminosis A.
warnings.thiamineRiskRawFish: Raw freshwater fish destroys thiamine via thiaminase. Cook fish for cats or use saltwater species occasionally.
warnings.steatitisTunaExcess: Tuna exceeds 10% of intake. Mercury accumulation + steatitis (yellow fat disease) risk. Rotate proteins.

nutrition.toastCaution: Plan ready — check the warnings below.
nutrition.toastFail: Plan is below targets — see the warnings below.

nutrition.placeholderHint_cat: Enter your cat's profile and tap Calculate.
nutrition.exportFooter: PawCook — not veterinary advice. Consult your veterinarian before major diet changes.

foodSafety.title: Food Safety Reference
foodSafety.subtitle: Search safe and toxic foods for dogs.
foodSafety.noResultsHint: Nothing here matched "{{term}}". Check the spelling or try a shorter word — toxic foods are under their own tab.
foodSafety.subtitle_cat: Search safe and toxic foods for cats. Cats are more sensitive than dogs to many compounds.

supplements.title: Supplement Guide
supplements.subtitle: A homemade diet without bones requires calcium supplementation — the #1 deficiency in DIY dog food.
supplements.caRatio: Ca:P ratio target: 1.2:1 to 1.4:1 (AAFCO acceptable range 1:1 to 2.1:1). Meat alone is ~1:20 — heavily phosphorus-skewed. Supplementation is mandatory.

bal.balanceItFeline: UC Davis spinoff — feline-specific formulation service and premixes.
bal.ezComplete: EZ Complete Fur Cats — all-in-one premix designed for raw and cooked homemade feline diets.
bal.alnutrin: Alnutrin with calcium — premix with optional Ca, designed for boneless homemade cat food.

aafcoTable.title: AAFCO Nutrient Profile
aafcoTable.source: AAFCO Dog Food Nutrient Profiles (dry-matter basis)

ca_p.label: Calcium : Phosphorus
ca_p.note: Critical for skeletal health; growing puppies should stay below 1.6:1

omega.label: Omega-6 : Omega-3
omega.note: Lower ratios (5:1 to 10:1) reduce systemic inflammation

aafcoTable.sourceLine: AAFCO Dog Food Nutrient Profile — dry matter basis
aafcoTable.sourceLine_cat: AAFCO Cat Food Nutrient Profile — dry matter basis

transition.title: 7-day transition from kibble to fresh food
transition.summary: Gradual transition prevents osmotic diarrhea and gastric upset. Use a single highly digestible protein (chicken or turkey) for the entire transition.

0.label: Day 1–2
0.note: Watch stool — looser stool is normal, but firm should be the baseline by day 2.

1.label: Day 3–4
1.note: If digestive issues appear, hold this ratio an extra day before advancing.

2.label: Day 5–6
2.note: Begin tapering off any commercial food.

3.label: Day 7+
3.note: Full fresh diet. Introduce protein rotation only after 14–21 days of stable digestion.

tips.0: Add a teaspoon of plain canned pumpkin for the first 3 days to support digestion.
tips.1: Hold off on supplements (calcium, fish oil) for the first 48 hours, then introduce one at a time.
tips.2: If diarrhea persists more than 48 hours, return to the previous day's ratio and consult a vet.
tips.3: Senior dogs and dogs with sensitive GI tracts may need a 10–14 day transition instead of 7.

supplements.taurineCallout: Cats cannot synthesize taurine — supplementation is mandatory in cooked diets and strongly recommended in raw diets without hearts. Deficiency causes irreversible blindness and dilated cardiomyopathy.
supplements.subtitle_cat: Homemade cat diets without taurine, calcium, vitamin E and B-complex fail within months. Supplementation is mandatory, not optional.

hero.subtitle: Open-source tools for home-cooking dog owners — safe cooking times, daily nutrition plans, food safety guides, and supplement advice.
hero.subtitle_cat: Open-source tools for home-cooking cat owners — taurine-aware nutrition plans, safe cooking, food safety guides, and the supplement stack cats need.
hero.subtitleHousehold: Dogs and cats. One bag, one cook session, one plan you'll actually follow. We handle the macros, the safety temps, and the supplements.

features.heading: Everything you need
features.cooking: Safe cooking times & temperatures for 12 meat types via sous-vide, oven, stovetop, or slow cooker.
features.nutrition: Daily portion calculator based on your dog's weight, age, activity level, and diet.
features.foodSafety: Searchable database of toxic foods, safe meats, vegetables, and fruits.
features.supplements: Calcium, omega-3, and micronutrient guide for homemade diets.
features.nutrition_cat: Daily portion calculator based on your cat's weight, life stage and activity — built for obligate carnivores.

open.title: Free & open source
open.text: MIT licensed. Built by dog owners for dog owners.

bento.nutrition: Daily macros, calories and Ca:P balance, tuned to your dog.
bento.cooking: Safe time & temperature for every meat.
bento.foodSafety: Toxic foods, safe meats, vegetables, fruits.

how.heading: Your household, fed in three steps

step1.title: Add your household
step1.desc: One profile per pet — dogs, cats, or both. Weight, age, activity, allergies.
step1.desc_cat: One profile per pet — dogs, cats, or both. Weight, age, activity, allergies.

step2.title: Generate one plan
step2.desc: Simple meals by default: one protein per day, shared across pets. Sous-vide, oven, stovetop — your pick.
step2.desc_cat: Simple meals by default: one protein per day, shared across pets. Sous-vide, oven, stovetop — your pick.

step3.title: Cook one bag, dose the rest
step3.desc: Sous-vide a single household bag; follow the daily supplement card for what the bag doesn't cover.
step3.title_cat: Cook one bag, dose the rest
step3.desc_cat: Sous-vide a single household bag; follow the daily supplement card (taurine + cod liver oil) for what the bag doesn't cover.

how.heading_cat: Your household, fed in three steps

diets.heading: Or pick your own diet
diets.sub: Every macronutrient profile maps to AAFCO targets and your dog's life stage.
diets.sub_cat: Every cat profile maps to AAFCO feline targets and your cat's life stage. Taurine is non-negotiable.
diets.subPower: Simple meals is the default. Turn it off and you get five profiles per species, all AAFCO-aligned, all surfaced behind a single toggle.

mantra.body: The plan you'll actually cook beats the plan that's perfectly balanced on paper.
mantra.elab: PawCook's job is to make nutritionally adequate food easy to put on the floor — not to make nutritionally optimal food the owner abandons by week two.

story.heading: One plan a real kitchen can execute
story.sub: The full nutritional engine is still there. It just stops being the default. What you get is a plan that survives Tuesday at 7 a.m.

col1.body: 2 dogs + 1 cat. Same beef on beef day.
col1.caption: The household is the unit, not the pet.

col2.body: One protein, one cook session, one fridge window.
col2.caption: The cook flow drives the meal plan, not the reverse.

col3.body: Taurine + cod liver oil cover what the bag doesn't.
col3.caption: Surface deficits loudly; never simulate them silently.

whatsNew.list: Followability Mandate · Block rotation · Simple meals · Supplement card · Plan-aware cooking · Edit + rename plans

preview.heading: The actual product, not a marketing render
preview.sub: Four slices of the app — built from the same components you use after signing in. No fake screenshots.

plan.caption: Each meal card shows grams + kcal. Supplement card sits up top — every deficit is named, every dose is exact.
plan.principle: Surface deficits loudly; never simulate them silently.

cooking.caption: One bag per protein serve. Cook date · serve days · use by · pet tags — all derived from the plan, not fabricated.
cooking.principle: The cook flow drives the meal plan, not the reverse.

bag.caption: "3 bags × 0.66 kg — one bag per chicken serve." Merge adjacent serves when fridge-safe to skip cook sessions.
bag.principle: Sub-threshold add-ins are pills, not ingredients.

wizard.caption: "Cook ahead" with live stats: bags · cook sessions · sessions saved vs daily. See the frugal payoff before you generate.
wizard.principle: Followability is a nutritional input, not a UX nicety.

cooking.title: Cooking plan
cooking.summary: 4 bags · 4 cook sessions · one protein per bag

bag.summary: {{bags}} {{meat}} bag(s) across {{days}} days for {{pets}} pets. Cook all {{kg}} kg at once — fridge 1 bag, freeze the rest.

wizard.simpleMealsShort: One protein per meal · daily supplement card covers the gap

about.title: About PawCook
about.subtitle: Open-source dog food cooking calculator built for home-cooking dog owners.
about.notVetAdvice: This tool is not veterinary advice. It provides general guidelines based on published veterinary nutrition standards (AAFCO, NRC, FEDIAF). Every dog is individual. For long-term homemade feeding, consult a board-certified veterinary nutritionist (ACVN diplomate).
about.noBones: Cooked bones are never safe. This tool will never recommend cooked bones in any recipe.
about.pathogen: Pathogen safety: Cook to safe internal temperatures — chicken & turkey 74°C, pork 71°C, ground meat 71°C, fish 63°C — verified with a meat thermometer. Cool cooked food promptly.
about.openSourceText: PawCook is MIT licensed. Contributions, corrections, and feature requests welcome on
about.privacyLead: PawCook stores nothing on our servers — because we don't have any. The app is a static page served by GitHub Pages.
about.privacyNoTracking: No analytics, no tracking pixels, no third-party scripts, no cookies. We do not know who you are, what you searched, or which recipe you cooked.
about.privacyStorageBody: Your browser's localStorage keeps your theme, language and the last values you entered in each calculator — so you don't have to re-enter them next time. That data never leaves your device.
about.privacyStorageKeys: Stored keys: pawcook_theme, i18nextLng, pawcook_cooking_input, pawcook_nutrition_input.
about.privacyClearBody: Open your browser's site-data settings for this site and clear everything. PawCook will reset to defaults the next time you load it.
about.privacyHostBody: PawCook is hosted on GitHub Pages. To serve the page, GitHub's infrastructure necessarily sees your IP address. We have no access to those logs and no other relationship with you.
about.subtitle_cat: Open-source homemade cat and dog food calculator built for people who cook for their pets.
about.notVetAdvice_cat: This tool is not veterinary advice. It provides general guidelines based on published veterinary nutrition standards (AAFCO, NRC, FEDIAF). Cats in particular have tight margins for taurine, arachidonic acid and vitamin A. For long-term homemade feeding, consult a board-certified veterinary nutritionist (ACVN diplomate).

dog.nrc: NRC Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats (2006)

cat.nrc: NRC Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats (2006)
cat.aaha: AAHA / AAFP 2021 Feline Life Stage Guidelines
cat.zoran: Zoran (2002) — The Carnivore Connection to Nutrition in Cats

lamb.label: Lamb
lamb.notes: Novel protein; good for beef-sensitive dogs

salmon.label: Salmon
salmon.notes: Raw only if frozen ≥7 days first. Watch Neorickettsia (Pacific NW).

pumpkin.label: Pumpkin (plain)
pumpkin.notes: No sugar/spice — never pumpkin pie filling

apple.label: Apple (no seeds/core)
apple.notes: Fiber, Vit C — seeds contain cyanide

onions_garlic.label: Onions, Garlic, Leeks, Chives, Scallions
onions_garlic.effect: Destroys red blood cells → hemolytic anemia

avocado.label: Avocado
avocado.effect: Vomiting, diarrhea; pit is choking + obstruction risk

green_tomatoes.label: Tomatoes (green/leaves)
green_tomatoes.effect: Tremors, GI issues — ripe red flesh usually OK

alcohol.label: Alcohol
alcohol.effect: Same as humans but much smaller dose lethal

salt_excess.label: Salt (excess)
salt_excess.effect: Sodium ion poisoning — vomiting, tremors, seizures

dairy_milk.label: Milk & Lactose Dairy
dairy_milk.effect: Most adult dogs are lactose intolerant — diarrhea, gas

raw_egg_whites.label: Raw Egg Whites (regular feeding)
raw_egg_whites.effect: Long-term feeding binds biotin → skin/coat issues. Whole raw eggs OK occasionally.

hops.label: Hops (brewing)
hops.effect: Malignant hyperthermia, fatal in some breeds

raw_freshwater_fish.label: Raw Freshwater Fish (staple)
raw_freshwater_fish.effect: Destroys thiamine (B1) → neurological damage, seizures. Cooking inactivates the enzyme. Occasional small amounts of cooked freshwater fish are OK.

tuna_staple.label: Tuna as a Staple
tuna_staple.effect: Mercury accumulation; steatitis (yellow fat disease) from polyunsaturated fats without sufficient vitamin E. Cap at ≤ 10% of intake and rotate.

liver_excess_cat.label: Liver in Excess
liver_excess_cat.effect: Vitamin A toxicity (hypervitaminosis A) — bone deformation, joint disease. Cap liver at 5–10 g/day for a 4 kg cat. Cats are uniquely sensitive.

dog_food_for_cat.label: Dog Food Long-Term
dog_food_for_cat.compound: Missing taurine + arachidonic acid + preformed vitamin A
dog_food_for_cat.effect: Not acutely toxic, but lethally inadequate over months. Cats fed dog food develop dilated cardiomyopathy, retinal degeneration, and fatty liver.

lilies_environment.label: Lilies (all parts, environment)
lilies_environment.effect: Not a food, but household contact is enough — even pollen on fur, ingested while grooming, causes acute kidney failure within 36–72h. Remove all lilies (Lilium and Hemerocallis) from any home with a cat.

vegan_diet_cat.label: Plant-Only / Vegan Diet
vegan_diet_cat.effect: Cats lack the enzymes to derive arachidonic acid, taurine, vitamin A, vitamin D, and arginine from plant sources. Vegan cat diets are not safe at any duration.

calcium.label: Calcium
calcium.target: ~500 mg per 454g (1 lb) of meat, OR 9–15 mg per kcal

eggshell.label: Ground eggshell
eggshell.dose: ½ tsp per 1 lb meat (1 large eggshell ≈ 2000 mg Ca)

bone_meal.label: Bone meal (food grade)
bone_meal.dose: ½ tsp per 1 lb meat (verify lead-free)

omega3.label: Omega-3 (EPA + DHA)
omega3.target: ~300 mg combined EPA+DHA per 9–13 kg body weight on non-fish days

fish_oil.label: Fish oil
fish_oil.dose: Varies by concentration — store refrigerated, replace every 2 months once opened

sardines.label: Sardines (whole, in water)
sardines.dose: 1 sardine per 10 lb body weight, 2–3× weekly

cod_liver_oil.label: Cod liver oil
cod_liver_oil.dose: Contains Vit A & D too — don't double-dose

cod_liver_oil_d.label: Cod liver oil or supplement
cod_liver_oil_d.dose: Per label — do not exceed 3000 IU/kg DM

welcome.title: Who are you cooking for?
welcome.body: Cats and dogs need different nutrition. Pick one to start — you can switch any time from the top bar.
welcome.dogDesc: Dogs are flexible omnivores with moderate protein needs.
welcome.catDesc: Cats are obligate carnivores. Their plans are stricter.
welcome.footnote: PawCook is not veterinary advice. Consult a vet nutritionist for chronic conditions or growth.
welcome.decideLater: Decide later — show me the app

errorBoundary.title: Something went wrong
errorBoundary.description: The page failed to load. Reload to try again, or reset locally-stored data if the problem persists.

notFound.title: Page not found
notFound.description: The page you’re looking for doesn’t exist or was moved.
